docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
To help you speak the language of spreadsheets, here are the essential terms used throughout this guide.

Term Definition Cell The intersection of a vertical column and a horizontal row. Each cell is identified by a coordinate (e.g., A1). Range A collection of two or more cells. For example, A1:A10 refers to all cells from A1 down to A10. Function A predefined command (like SUM or AVERAGE) that performs a specific calculation. Header The top row of a table used to identify the contents of the columns below it. Value The actual data stored in a cell, which can be a number, text, or a date. Cloud-Based Software that runs on the internet rather than being installed on your hard drive, allowing for real-time saving and sharing.

docs.title: Task 1: Creating a Basic Automatic Sum
Overview
docs.text:
In Google Sheets, formulas allow you to perform calculations automatically. One of the most common formulas is SUM(), which adds a group of numbers together. Instead of calculating totals manually, you can let Google Sheets update the result instantly whenever the data changes.

This concept is foundational because every spreadsheet task builds on the idea of using formulas to automate work.

docs.title: What is a Formula ?
docs.text:
A formula in Google Sheets is an expression that begins with an equals sign (=) and instructs the spreadsheet to compute a value.

The most common function is the SUM() function which accepts a range of cells and returns their total.

For example:

=SUM(A1:A3)

This tells Google Sheets: “Add the values from A1 through A3.”

Using formulas ensures your totals stay correct even when the data changes without any recalculations or mistakes.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Suppose you have three numbers in cells A1, A2, and A3:

Cell Value A1 10 A2 20 A3 30

Entering the formula:

=SUM(A1:A3)

This will produce the result 60 in the cell where the formula is placed.

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text:
This task teaches you how to let the software do math for you so you never have to use a handheld calculator for a list of numbers again.

  1. Click on cell A1.
  2. Type the number 10 and press Enter.
  3. In cell A2, type the number 20 and press Enter.
  4. In cell A3, type the number 30 and press Enter.
  5. Click on cell A4 (this is where your total will go).
  6. Type the equals sign = on your keyboard.
  7. Type the word SUM in all caps.
  8. Type an opening parenthesis (.
  9. Click and drag your mouse from cell A1 down to A3.
  10. Type a closing parenthesis ) and press Enter.

Your total will now appear in A4, and it will update automatically if any of the values in A1–A3 change.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Formulas always begin with = . Without it, Google Sheets will treat the text as plain data.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
When working with large datasets, it can be difficult for the eye to track a single row of information from left to right. Alternating colors, also known as "banded rows," apply different shades to every other row to improve readability and give your spreadsheet a professional, polished look.

This task is essential for data presentation. By using the built-in formatting tools, you ensure that even if you add more rows later, the styling remains consistent and clean.

docs.title: What is a Banded Row?
docs.text: A banded row is a formatting style where the background color of even-numbered rows differs from odd-numbered rows. In Google Sheets, this is handled through the Alternating colors menu, which automates the shading process so you don't have to color cells manually.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Imagine a list of 50 employees and their phone numbers. Without banding, the white background makes the lines blend together. After applying Alternating colors:

  • Row 1: Dark Blue (Header)
  • Row 2: Light Blue
  • Row 3: White
  • Row 4: Light Blue

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text:
1. Click and drag your mouse to highlight the specific range of cells that contains your data.
  2. Move your cursor to the top Menu Bar.
  3. Click on the Format tab to open the dropdown menu.
  4. Scroll down and select Alternating colors.
  5. Observe the Alternating colors settings panel that appears on the right side of the screen.
  6. Under the Styles section, click on a color preset (e.g., Blue, Green, or Grey) that matches your preference.
  7. Check the box labeled Header if your top row contains titles or labels.
  8. Check the box labeled Footer if you want the very last row to have a distinct summary color.
  9. Review the "Apply to range" box at the top of the panel to ensure it covers your entire dataset.
  10. Click the Done button at the bottom of the side panel to save your changes.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: As spreadsheets grow in length, the top row (which usually contains titles like "Date" or "Price") disappears as you scroll down. Freezing the header row locks it at the top of your screen, ensuring you always know exactly what data you are looking at, regardless of how far down you scroll.

docs.title: What is "Freezing"?
docs.text: Freezing a row acts like a physical pin; it holds a specific section of the spreadsheet in place while the rest of the grid remains scrollable. This is a "view-only" change, meaning it doesn't affect how the sheet prints or how formulas work—it only changes how you see the data.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: If you have a list of 200 grocery items, by the time you reach item #150, you might forget if Column C is "Unit Price" or "Total Cost." By Freezing Row 1, the titles stay visible at the top of your browser window at all times.

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text:
1. Move your mouse to the far left of the screen and click the number 1 to highlight the entire first row.
  2. Move your cursor to the top Menu Bar.
  3. Click on the View tab.
  4. Hover your mouse over the first option, Freeze.
  5. In the side menu that appears, click on 1 row.
  6. Look closely at the line between Row 1 and Row 2; you will notice a thick grey divider line has appeared.
  7. Click on any empty cell in the middle of your sheet to deselect the row.
  8. Use your mouse wheel or trackpad to scroll down toward Row 50 or 100.
  9. Observe that Row 1 stays fixed at the top of the screen while the other numbers scroll underneath it.
  10. To unlock the row later, navigate back to View > Freeze > No rows.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Data imported from websites or other documents often contains "hidden" spaces at the beginning or end of text. These invisible characters can cause formulas to fail and make your sorting look messy. The Trim whitespace tool scans your text and instantly deletes these unnecessary spaces.

docs.title: What is Whitespace?
docs.text: In computing, "whitespace" refers to any character that represents horizontal or vertical space (like hitting the spacebar or the tab key). While invisible, Google Sheets treats "Apple" and " Apple " (with a space) as two completely different pieces of data. Trimming ensures your data is uniform and "clean."

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• Original Data: $150.00
  • After Trim: $150.00 The extra gaps are removed, but the space between words (like "John Smith") is preserved.

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text:
1. Click on cell B1 to select it for this exercise.
  2. Press the Spacebar three times, type the word Hello, and press the Spacebar three more times.
  3. Press Enter to commit the text to the cell.
  4. Click back on cell B1—notice how the text looks off-center because of the extra spaces.
  5. Move your cursor to the top Menu Bar.
  6. Click on the Data tab.
  7. Hover your mouse over the Data cleanup option.
  8. Select Trim whitespace from the sub-menu.
  9. Wait for the pop-up notification to appear, telling you how many cells were cleaned.
  10. Click OK on the notification to return to your perfectly formatted cell.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Welcome to the Google Sheets User Documentation. This guide will help you get started with some of the features and functionality of Google Sheets.

docs.title: What is Google Sheets?
docs.text:
Google Sheets is a web-based software that allows you to create, organize, analyze and share data. Unlike traditional software, it lives in the cloud, meaning multiple people can work on the same document simultaneously. It is primarily used for:

  • Data Organization: Storing information in a structured grid of rows and columns.
  • Calculations: Using built-in formulas and functions to process numerical data.
  • Visualization: Turning raw numbers into charts, graphs and pivot tables
  • Collaboration: Working in real-time with team members on the same document.

docs.title: Purpose of This Guide
docs.text: The instruction tasks in this documentation are designed to help users practice essential Google Sheets skills while also testing the clarity and accuracy of the documentation. By completing tasks such as creating sums, applying formatting, freezing headers, and cleaning data, users can confirm that each procedure is understandable, repeatable, and effective.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text: This guide is written for users with little to no experience. It is assumed that you have a basic familiarity with using a web browser and google account. No prior experience with computer programming or advanced math principles is required. This guide will build your knowledge from the ground up, starting with basic cell entry and moving toward more complicated data relationships.

docs.title: Documentation Conventions
docs.text: Convention Meaning Example Bold UI elements, buttons, or menu items. Click File > Share. Monospace Formulas, functions, or text to be typed. Enter =SUM(A1:A10) into the cell. Italics Important terms or document titles. Refer to the Glossary of Terms for more. [Bracketed] Keyboard shortcuts. Press [Ctrl] + [C] to copy.

docs.title: Admonitions and Alerts
docs.text:
Throughout the instructions, you ill see specialized callouts. These are designed to keep you safe from data loss and provide tips for efficiency.

💡 TIP: You can quickly fill as series of dates by clicking and dragging the blue square at the bottom-right corner of a selected cell.

⚠️ CAUTION: Deleting a sheet tab is permanent. Always ensure you have a backup or check the Version History before removing large blocks of data.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text:
Even for experienced users, spreadsheets can occasionally behave unexpectedly. If you run into trouble, check the solutions below.

If you see/experience... It likely means... Try this solution... A red triangle in the cell corner There is a formula error. Hover your mouse over the cell to read the specific error message (e.g., a typo in your formula). The formula displays as text (e.g., you see "=SUM...") The cell is formatted as "Plain Text" or you forgot the equals sign. 1. Ensure the formula starts with =. 2. Go to Format > Number and select Automatic. #REF! Error A cell referenced in your formula was deleted. Press [Ctrl] + [Z] to undo the deletion, or click the cell to update the range manually. "Alternating colors" is greyed out You haven't selected a range of cells. Click and drag your mouse to highlight a group of cells first, then try the Format menu again. A "Loading..." message that won't go away Your internet connection is unstable. Refresh your browser page. Because Google Sheets is cloud-based, it saves every few seconds, so you won't lose your work.
